questions.text: Statement:The availability of imported fruits has increased in the indigenous market and so the demand for indigenous fruits has been decreased. Courses of Action:I. To help the indigenous producers of fruits, the Government should impose a high import duty on these fruits, even if they are not of good quality.II. The fruit vendors should stop selling imported fruits. So that the demand for indigenous fruits would increase.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: This option is wrong because Course I suggests supporting low-quality fruits, which is unreasonable and harmful to consumers.
This option is wrong because stopping vendors from selling imported fruits is impractical and against free market choice.

The ideas suggested in both courses of action I and II represent unfair means to cut competition. The correct way would be to devise methods and techniques such that the indigenous producers could produce better-quality fruits and make them available in the market at prices comparable with those of the imported ones. Hence, neither I nor II follow.
This option is wrong because neither course is practical or logical.

questions.explanation: The ideas suggested in both I and II represent unfair means to cut competition. The correct way would be to devise methods and techniques such that the indigenous producers could produce better-quality fruits and make them available in the market at prices comparable with those of the imported ones. Hence, neither I nor II follow.

questions.text: Statement: A severe drought is reported to have been set in several parts of the country. Courses of Action:I. The government should immediately make arrangements for providing financial assistance to those affected.II. Food, water, and fodder should immediately be sent to all these areas to save the people and cattle.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Wrong because financial help alone is not enough; immediate supply of food, water, and fodder is also necessary in a drought.
Wrong because relief materials alone are insufficient; affected people also need financial assistance for recovery.
Wrong because both actions can and should be taken together; they are not alternatives.
Correct because both financial aid and immediate supply of essentials are practical, necessary, and urgent responses to a severe drought.

questions.explanation: In a severe drought, the immediate priorities are life sustaining supplies like food and water (Action II). Financial assistance (Action I) is also a valid and necessary step to help affected people manage other needs and begin recovery. Both courses of action are logical responses.

questions.text: Statement: A large number of people die every year due to drinking polluted water during the summer.Courses of Action:I. The government should make adequate arrangements to provide safe drinking water to all its citizens.II. The people should be educated about the dangers of drinking polluted water.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Providing safe drinking water is essential, but alone it may not suffice if people are unaware of the risks. Hence, this option is not fully adequate.
Educating people is crucial, but without access to safe water, awareness alone cannot solve the problem. Therefore, this option does not fully address the issue.
Both actions are necessary in conjunction to effectively tackle the issue; hence, either one alone is insufficient.
Addressing the problem requires a dual approach: providing safe drinking water and raising awareness about the dangers of polluted water. Therefore, both actions are necessary.

questions.explanation: This problem requires a two-pronged approach. The government has a responsibility to provide a safe water supply (Action I), which is a long-term solution. Simultaneously, educating the public (Action II) empowers them to make safer choices and addresses the immediate risk.

questions.text: Statement: Most of those who study in premier engineering colleges in Pakistan migrate to developed nations for better prospects. Courses of Action:I. All students joining these colleges should be asked to sign a bond to remain in Pakistan for at least ten years after they complete their education.II. All those students who desire to settle in developed nations should be asked to pay the entire cost of their education which the government subsidizes.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Wrong because forcing students to sign a 10-year bond is unrealistic, restrictive, and violates personal freedom.
Wrong because asking students to repay the entire subsidized cost is extreme and impractical as a blanket rule.
Wrong because both actions are unreasonable, so choosing either one is not justified.
Correct because both courses of action are harsh, impractical, and not fair solutions to the problem of migration.

questions.explanation: The statement highlights that many engineering graduates leave Pakistan for better opportunities. Course of action I is impractical and unethical, as it restricts personal freedom without addressing the real reasons for migration. Course of action II is also flawed, as subsidies are meant to develop national talent, not penalize choices.

questions.text: Statement: As stated in the recent census report the female to male ratio is alarmingly low.Courses of Action:I. The government should conduct another census to verify the results.II. The government should immediately issue orders to all the departments to encourage people to improve the ratio.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Correct because conducting another census to verify alarming data is a logical and practical first step before taking major actions.
Wrong because issuing orders to “encourage people to improve the ratio” is vague, unrealistic, and not directly actionable.
Wrong because only one is reasonable; second does not logically follow.
Wrong because one is a sensible and valid response to the problem.

questions.explanation: The low female‑to‑male ratio needs verification before any action. Conducting another census (I) is sensible to confirm the data, while issuing orders to improve the ratio (II) is premature without accurate information. Thus Only I follows.

questions.text: Statement: There is an unprecedented increase in the migration of villagers to urban areas as repeated crop failure has put them into a precarious financial situation.Courses of Action:I. The villagers should be provided with an alternate source of income in their villages which will make them stay put.II. The migrated villagers should be provided with jobs in the urban areas to help them survive.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Correct because providing alternate sources of income in villages directly addresses the root cause (crop failure and financial distress) and helps prevent migration.
Wrong because providing jobs in cities accepts migration instead of solving the cause, and does not reduce rural distress.
Wrong because Course II is not an appropriate corrective action; only Course I is logical
Wrong because Course II does not solve the problem stated; hence both do not follow.

questions.explanation: Action I addresses the root cause of the problem (lack of income in villages) and is a sustainable, long-term solution. Action II is a temporary fix that could encourage more migration and further strain urban resources. Therefore, Action I is the more logical course.

questions.text: Statement: A recent study shows that children below five die in the cities of developing countries mainly from diarrhea and parasitic intestinal worms.Courses of Action:I. Governments of the developing countries should take adequate measures to improve the hygienic conditions in the cities.II. Children below five years in the cities of the developing countries need to be kept under periodic medical check-ups.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Clearly, the two diseases mentioned are caused by unhygienic conditions. So, improving the hygienic conditions is a step towards their eradication. Also, periodic medical check-up will help timely detection of the disease and hence a proper treatment. So, both I and II follow.

Clearly, the two diseases mentioned are caused by unhygienic conditions. So, improving the hygienic conditions is a step towards their eradication. Also, periodic medical check-up will help timely detection of the disease and hence a proper treatment. So, both I and II follow.

Clearly, the two diseases mentioned are caused by unhygienic conditions. So, improving the hygienic conditions is a step towards their eradication. Also, periodic medical check-up will help timely detection of the disease and hence a proper treatment. So, both I and II follow.

Clearly, the two diseases mentioned are caused by unhygienic conditions. So, improving the hygienic conditions is a step towards their eradication. Also, periodic medical check-up will help timely detection of the disease and hence a proper treatment. So, both I and II follow.

questions.explanation: Improving hygiene (Action I) is a crucial preventative measure that addresses the root cause of these diseases. Periodic medical check-ups (Action II) are an important curative and monitoring measure to treat existing cases and prevent deaths. Both actions are necessary.

questions.text: Statement: The car dealer found that there was a tremendous response for the new XYZ car, with long queues of people complaining about the duration of business hours and arrangements. Courses of Action: I. People should make their own arrangements for lunch and snacks while going for the booking and be ready to spend several hours. II. The dealer should make arrangements for more booking desks and increased business hours to serve more people in less time.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Seeing the tremendous response, the dealer must make suitable arrangements and deploy more personnel to take care of customers so that they don't have to wait excessively long for booking. So, only course II follows.

Seeing the tremendous response, the dealer must make suitable arrangements and deploy more personnel to take care of customers so that they don't have to wait excessively long for booking. So, only course II follows.

Seeing the tremendous response, the dealer must make suitable arrangements and deploy more personnel to take care of customers so that they don't have to wait excessively long for booking. So, only course II follows.

Seeing the tremendous response, the dealer must make suitable arrangements and deploy more personnel to take care of customers so that they don't have to wait excessively long for booking. So, only course II follows.

questions.explanation: Action I unfairly places the burden on the customers. The logical business response to high demand and customer complaints is to improve service by increasing capacity, such as adding more desks and extending hours (Action II).

questions.text: Statement: The Chairman stressed the need for making education system more flexible and regretted that the curriculum has not been revised in keeping with the pace of the changes taking place.Courses of Action:I. The curriculum should be reviewed and revised periodically.II. The system of education should be made more flexible.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Clearly, the situation demands making the education system more flexible and changing it periodically according to the needs of the time. So, both the courses follow.
Clearly, the situation demands making the education system more flexible and changing it periodically according to the needs of the time. So, both the courses follow.
Clearly, the situation demands making the education system more flexible and changing it periodically according to the needs of the time. So, both the courses follow.
Clearly, the situation demands making the education system more flexible and changing it periodically according to the needs of the time. So, both the courses follow.

questions.explanation: The statement explicitly identifies two problems: a need for flexibility and an outdated curriculum. Therefore, both courses of action directly address the issues raised by the Chairman and are logical follow-ups.

questions.text: Statement: There has been an unprecedented increase in the number of successful candidates in this year's School Leaving Certificate Examination.Courses of Action:I. The government should make arrangements to increase number of seats of intermediate courses in existing colleges.II. The government should take active steps to open new colleges to accommodate all these successful candidates.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: This is incorrect because increasing seats in existing colleges alone may not be enough to accommodate the unusually large number of successful candidates.
This is incorrect because opening new colleges takes time, and immediate arrangements like increasing seats in existing colleges are also necessary.
This is incorrect because both actions can be taken together; they are not mutually exclusive.
This is correct because increasing seats in existing colleges and opening new colleges are both practical and necessary steps to deal with the increased number of successful candidates.

questions.explanation: To accommodate a large, sudden increase in successful candidates, a combination of short-term and long-term solutions is needed. Increasing seats in existing colleges (Action I) is an immediate fix, while opening new colleges (Action II) is a necessary long-term strategy to handle sustained growth.

questions.text: Statement: The officer in charge of a company had a hunch that some money was missing from the safe. Courses of action:I. He should get it recounted with the help of the staff and check it with the balance sheet.II. He should inform the police.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: If only I is most appropriate
options.explanation: Course of Action I:Recounting the money and cross-checking with the balance sheet is a logical first step. The officer's suspicion needs verification before any further action.

options.text: If only II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Course of Action II:Informing the police at this stage is premature. Without verifying the suspicion, it could lead to unnecessary complications.

options.text: If either I or II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Neither action is interchangeable. Verification (I) should precede escalation (II).

options.text: If neither I nor II is most appropriate
options.explanation: One of the actions is indeed appropriate. Verification (I) is necessary to confirm the suspicion.

questions.explanation: The statement says the officer only has a "hunch." The most logical first step is to verify if money is actually missing by recounting it (Action I). Informing the police (Action II) would be premature and unprofessional without first confirming the loss.

questions.text: Statement: Japan is not likely to grant Pakistan's request for a $500 million loan for the current year. Courses of action:I. Pakistan should approach other countries to get a loan.II. Pakistan should continue to persuade Japan to grant the loan.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: If only I is most appropriate
options.explanation: If Japan is not likely to grant the loan, Pakistan should look for alternative sources. Approaching other countries is a practical and realistic course of action.

options.text: If only II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Continuing to persuade Japan is not appropriate because the statement already indicates that Japan is unlikely to grant the loan.

options.text: If either I or II is most appropriate
options.explanation: This is incorrect because only one action (approaching other countries) is suitable under the given situation.

options.text: If both I and II are most appropriate
options.explanation: This is incorrect because persisting with Japan despite low chances is not a wise or effective action.

questions.explanation: Given the unlikelihood of the statement says Japan is unlikely to grant the loan, so persuading Japan (II) is probably futile. Approaching other countries (I) is a practical alternative.

questions.text: Statement: The Librarian finds some cases in which pages from certain books issued from the library are torn. Courses of action:I. The Librarian should keep a record of books issued to each student, and if the pages are found torn, strict measures should be taken against the child who had been issued that book.II. Some funds should be collected from the children collectively to renovate the library.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: If only I is most appropriate
options.explanation: This option is correct because it directly addresses the issue of torn pages by holding the responsible party accountable, thus serving as a preventive measure.

options.text: If only II is most appropriate
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Renovating the library does not address the issue of torn pages and unfairly penalizes all students, regardless of their involvement.

options.text: If either I or II is most appropriate
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because both actions do not equally address the problem. Only I is effective in preventing further damage.

options.text: If neither I nor II is most appropriate
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as action I is an appropriate response to the problem identified in the statement.

questions.explanation: Action I directly addresses the problem by creating a system of accountability to deter future vandalism. Action II punishes all students, including those who are not responsible, and does not solve the root problem of torn pages.

questions.text: Statement: Most development plans exist on paper only. Courses of action: I. The incharges should be instructed to supervise the fieldwork regularly. II. The supply of paper to such departments should be cut short.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: If only I is the most appropriate
options.explanation: Course of Action I suggests increased supervision, which directly targets the problem of plans not being implemented practically.

options.text: If only II is the most appropriate
options.explanation: Course of Action II proposes reducing paper supply, which does not address the core issue of non-implementation and could lead to operational challenges.

options.text: If either I or II is most appropriate
options.explanation: This is incorrect because Course of Action I is the only one that effectively addresses the issue of implementation.

options.text: If neither I nor II is most appropriate
options.explanation: This is incorrect as Course of Action I provides a practical solution to the problem.

questions.explanation: The problem is a lack of implementation, not an excess of paper. Instructing supervisors to monitor fieldwork (Action I) directly addresses the gap between planning and execution. Cutting the paper supply (Action II) is an illogical and punitive measure that doesn't solve the problem.

questions.text: Statement: The Federal Investigation Agency (FIA) receives a complaint about an officer taking a bribe. Courses of action: I. The FIA should try to catch the officer red-handed and then take strict action against him.II. The FIA should wait for some more complaints about the officer to be sure about the matter.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: If only I is most appropriate
options.explanation: Course of Action I involves proactive steps to catch the officer in the act, ensuring irrefutable evidence and facilitating immediate corrective measures. This approach is direct and maintains the integrity of the investigation process.

options.text: If only II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Course of Action II suggests waiting for more complaints, which could allow unethical behavior to persist unchecked. It lacks immediacy and does not uphold the standards required for timely justice.

options.text: If either I or II is most appropriate
options.explanation: This option implies that both actions are equally effective, which is not accurate. Course of Action I is clearly more direct and effective in addressing the issue.

options.text: If neither I nor II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Suggesting that neither action is appropriate is incorrect, as Course of Action I provides a clear and effective solution to the problem.

questions.explanation: A complaint of corruption requires immediate and decisive action. Waiting for more complaints (Action II) could allow the corruption to continue. The most appropriate course of action is to investigate the current complaint and attempt to gather concrete evidence (Action I).

questions.text: Statement: Many medical and engineering graduates are taking up jobs in administration and banks. Courses of action:I. All professionals should be advised to refrain from taking up such jobs. II. The government should appoint a committee to find out the reasons for this trend and suggest remedial measures.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: If only I is most appropriate
options.explanation: Advising professionals to avoid these jobs doesn't solve the problem's root cause and may not be feasible.

options.text: If only II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Investigating through a government committee can uncover the reasons behind this trend, allowing for informed solutions.

options.text: If either I or II is most appropriate
options.explanation: This option is not suitable as both actions are not equally effective in addressing the issue.

options.text: If neither I nor II is most appropriate
options.explanation: One of the actions indeed provides a logical approach to tackling the problem, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: Simply advising professionals (Action I) is unlikely to be effective. To solve a complex socio-economic problem, it is first necessary to understand its root causes. Appointing a committee to investigate (Action II) is the most logical step toward developing effective, long-term solutions.

questions.text: Statement: Due to a substantial reduction in fares by airline services, a large number of passengers who were traveling by upper classes in trains have switched to airline services. Courses of action: I. The railways should immediately reduce the fare structure of the upper classes substantially to retain its passengers. II. The railways should reduce the capacity of upper classes in all trains to avoid loss.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: If only I is most appropriate
options.explanation: Reducing fares could help railways remain competitive and retain passengers who are drawn to cheaper airline tickets. It directly addresses the root of the issue: passenger loss due to fare differences.

options.text: If only II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Reducing the capacity of upper classes is a reactive measure that doesn't address the core issue of losing passengers to airlines. It might lead to underutilization and further financial loss if demand returns.

options.text: If either I or II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Both actions cannot independently be considered optimal because they address the problem from different angles. Reducing fares is proactive, while reducing capacity is reactive.

options.text: If neither I nor II is most appropriate
options.explanation: This option suggests that neither action addresses the problem adequately, which is not the case. Reducing fares directly tackles the reason for passenger loss.

questions.explanation: The problem is a loss of customers to a competitor due to price. The most direct way to address this is to become more price-competitive by reducing fares (Action I). Reducing capacity (Action II) accepts the loss of customers rather than trying to win them back.

questions.text: Statement: Despite the Principal's repeated warnings, a child was caught secretly exploding crackers in the school. Courses of action:I. All the crackers should be taken away from the child and he should be threatened not to do it again.II. The child should be severely punished for his wrong act.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: If only I is most appropriate
options.explanation: Too mild. The child ignored repeated warnings, so just taking crackers and threatening is not enough.

options.text: If only II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Correct. The act was dangerous and intentional, so strict punishment is needed.

options.text: If either I or II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Wrong. Only one strong action (II) fits the situation, not both.

options.text: If neither I nor II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Wrong. Some disciplinary action is clearly required.

questions.explanation: The child has already ignored "repeated warnings," so another threat (Action I) is unlikely to be effective. The situation has escalated to a point where a more significant disciplinary action (punishment) is required to address the deliberate and repeated defiance of safety rules.

questions.text: Statement: A large number of people in ward X of the city are diagnosed to be suffering from a fatal malaria type.Courses of Action:I. The city municipal authority should take immediate steps to carry out extensive fumigation in ward X.II. The people in the area should be advised to take steps to avoid mosquito bites.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: If only I is most appropriate
options.explanation: Immediate action like fumigation is essential to directly address the mosquito population, but without individual precautions, the risk remains high.

options.text: If only II is most appropriate
options.explanation: While advising personal protection is crucial for individual safety, it doesn't tackle the root cause of the mosquito population effectively.

options.text: If either I or II is most appropriate
options.explanation: These actions are not alternatives; they are complementary and necessary together for a comprehensive response.

options.text: If both I and II are most appropriate
options.explanation: A combined approach of fumigation and personal protection is essential for effectively managing the outbreak and minimizing further spread.

questions.explanation: A public health crisis requires a comprehensive response. Fumigation (I) tackles the vector population, while personal protection (II) reduces immediate transmission risk. Both are essential and appropriate actions.

questions.text: Statement: Certain mining industries in Gilgit may come to a standstill because of a notification banning mining operations within 25 km of National Parks. Courses of action:I. The Department should be asked to immediately withdraw the notification.II. The government should make an effort to shift the parks and sanctuaries to other non-mining areas.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.text: If only I is most appropriate
options.explanation: Withdrawing the notification would harm environmental protection and defeat the purpose of safeguarding national parks.

options.text: If only II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Shifting parks and sanctuaries is impractical and environmentally unsound.

options.text: If either I or II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Both actions are inappropriate and unrealistic, so this option is incorrect.

options.text: If neither I nor II is most appropriate
options.explanation: Environmental protection must be maintained, and neither withdrawing the ban nor shifting parks is a suitable solution.

questions.explanation: Withdrawing a notification designed to protect the environment (Action I) is an irresponsible solution. Shifting entire national parks and ecosystems (Action II) is practically impossible and illogical. Neither course of action is a viable solution to the problem.

questions.text: Statement: City roads are flooded after a moderate rainfall. Courses of Action: I. Stormwater drainage system needs immediate repair. II. Ban construction of new roads.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Repairing the stormwater drainage system directly addresses the issue of flooding caused by rain.
Banning construction of new roads does not directly address the issue of existing roads flooding.
Only the repair of the stormwater drainage system is a relevant course of action here. Banning new road construction does not solve the flooding issue.
Repairing the drainage system is necessary to prevent flooding, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Flooding after moderate rain is a clear indicator that the drainage system is not functioning correctly. Repairing the system (Action I) is a direct and logical solution to the problem. Banning new construction (Action II) is an extreme measure that does not address the existing issue

questions.text: Statement: The number of cybercrimes has doubled in the last year. Courses of Action: I. Stricter cyber laws should be enforced. II. All internet users should be banned temporarily.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Enforcing stricter cyber laws is a practical and reasonable approach to tackle the increase in cybercrimes. It aims to deter potential criminals and ensure better regulation and protection of internet users.

Banning all internet users temporarily is an extreme and impractical measure that would disrupt essential services and infringe on individual rights without effectively targeting the issue of cybercrime.
While enforcing stricter cyber laws is a sound approach, banning all internet users is not a feasible solution. Therefore, both actions do not follow.
While banning all internet users is not appropriate, enforcing stricter cyber laws is a valid course of action. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Enforcing stricter laws (Action I) is a rational and targeted approach to deterring and punishing criminal activity. Banning all internet users (Action II) is an impractical and disproportionate response that would punish the innocent along with the guilty.

questions.text: Statement: The attendance in government schools is decreasing steadily. Courses of Action: I. Improve infrastructure and quality of teaching in schools. II. Make school uniforms compulsory.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Improving infrastructure and quality of teaching directly addresses the issue of decreasing attendance by potentially attracting more students. Thus, only I follows.
Making school uniforms compulsory does not directly address the underlying issue of decreasing attendance and may not have a significant impact on attracting students to attend school.
While improving infrastructure is relevant, making uniforms compulsory is not directly related to increasing attendance. Therefore, both actions do not follow.
This option is incorrect as improving infrastructure and quality of teaching is a valid course of action to address the issue of decreasing attendance.

questions.explanation: Decreasing attendance is likely due to issues with the quality of education or the school environment. Improving infrastructure and teaching (Action I) directly addresses these potential root causes. Making uniforms compulsory (Action II) is unlikely to have a significant impact on attendance.

questions.text: Statement: Cases of cheating in online exams are increasing. Courses of Action: I. Conduct exams in proctored physical centers. II. Cancel all online exams permanently.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Conducting exams in proctored physical centers can effectively reduce cheating by providing better supervision, making it a feasible solution.
Canceling all online exams permanently is an extreme measure and not a practical solution to address the cheating issue.
While option I is a practical solution, option II is too extreme. Both do not logically follow as solutions together.
Option I provides a feasible solution, so it's incorrect to say neither follows.

questions.explanation: Conducting exams in supervised physical centers (Action I) is a direct and effective way to mitigate cheating. Canceling all online exams permanently (Action II) is an extreme overreaction that ignores the benefits of online assessment.

questions.text: Statement: Farmers are protesting against new agricultural policies. Courses of Action: I. Government should hold talks with farmer representatives. II. Force should be used to end the protest.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Engaging in dialogue with the farmers can help address their concerns and potentially lead to a peaceful resolution.
Using force to end the protest could escalate the situation, leading to unrest and possibly violence, which is not a constructive solution.
While holding talks is a constructive approach, using force is generally not advisable in resolving protests as it can worsen the situation.
Engaging in talks is a reasonable and often necessary step in addressing grievances, thus this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: In a democratic society, the most appropriate response to a protest is to engage in dialogue to understand and address the protesters' concerns (Action I). Using force (Action II) is an extreme measure that escalates conflict and is not a constructive solution.

questions.text: Statement: A large number of youth are addicted to online gaming. Courses of Action: I. Introduce digital detox programs in schools and colleges. II. Ban all online games.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Introducing digital detox programs is a constructive approach to help youth manage their time and reduce addiction to online gaming.
Banning all online games is an extreme measure that may not be practical or enforceable and could lead to other unintended consequences.
While introducing digital detox programs is a good course of action, banning all online games is not a balanced or feasible approach.
This option is incorrect because introducing digital detox programs is a valid course of action to address the issue.

questions.explanation: Introducing educational and support programs (Action I) is a constructive approach to help people manage their behavior. A complete ban on all online games (Action II) is an impractical and overly drastic measure that is likely to be unenforceable.
